source.title: How do mammillary body inputs contribute to anterior thalamic function?

confidence_rationale: Strong, specific claim about mammillary body function that reframes the Papez circuit from a serial loop into two parallel streams (medial body / memory via ventral tegmental nucleus of Gudden; lateral body / head direction via dorsal tegmental nucleus). Directly supports the write-gating hypothesis because the two streams converge on anterior thalamus but carry different information types -- exactly the architecture expected if different action modes route through different Papez subpaths. Source quality is high (rigorous review with lesion, electrophysiology, and tracer evidence); mapping fidelity is high for the two-stream architectural claim; transfer risk is moderate (rodent data, but anterior-thalamic anatomy is well-conserved).
failure_signatures: If the mammillary bodies are a pure hippocampal relay (the pre-2015 consensus the review overturns), then the REE write-gating hypothesis loses its anatomical substrate for non-hippocampal control signals entering the Papez loop.

The two-stream architecture predicts that selective lesions to VTN-Gudden should impair memory write without impairing head-direction tracking; selective lesions to DTN-Gudden should do the opposite. If this double dissociation fails, the two-stream model (and its REE mapping) is weakened.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The mammillary bodies are not a passive hippocampal relay. They receive substantial non-hippocampal input from the ventral and dorsal tegmental nuclei of Gudden. The medial mammillary body (VTN-Gudden input) supports memory processes via the anterior medial thalamic nucleus; the lateral mammillary body (DTN-Gudden input) generates head-direction signals via the anterior dorsal thalamic nucleus. These two parallel streams converge on anterior thalamus and support distinct aspects of spatial memory.
mapping.ree_translation: The mammillary-anterior-thalamic relay in the Papez circuit is dual-channel, not single-channel. This is the structural substrate for mode-specific write profiles: different action modes (or different latent streams) can route through different Papez sub-pathways. Candidate mapping: z_world-domain hippocampal proposals route through the medial-mammillary / AM-thalamus channel; egocentric / head-direction signals route through the lateral-mammillary / AD-thalamus channel. The two channels can be independently gated.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper does not identify any specific write-gating signal. The two-stream architecture is necessary for mode-specific gating but not sufficient to prove it -- the gate-bearing projection remains unidentified. Rodent anatomy; human homology is well-established for mammillary bodies but the Gudden tegmental nuclei are less well-characterised in humans.
mapping.source_context: Review, primarily rodent lesion and electrophysiology literature; some non-human primate data.

confidence_components.notes: Review cites strong primary literature; mapping to write-gating is architectural (direct) rather than functional (inferential); transfer risk is normal for rodent-to-human anatomy in a well-conserved subcortical system.
